research_goal: To develop a memory management system for long-horizon agentic reasoning that achieves adaptive information density through visual layout, enabling more efficient utilization of limited context budgets by decoupling semantic content from token cost.

current_progress.text: Visual memory with adaptive information density improves long-horizon reasoning under tight context budgets compared to textual memory paradigms.
current_progress.key_result: The proposed method outperforms text-based baselines across all settings, achieving 74.6% average accuracy vs. 67.8% for the strongest agentic memory baseline at 1024 tokens on 10K context. Under extreme budgets (16 tokens), the proposed method maintains 62.2% accuracy while the strongest agentic memory baseline drops to 31.6%, representing a 30.6 percentage point improvement and approximately 8× improvement in effective context utilization.
current_progress.citation: Section 4.2 (Overall Performance - RQ1), Table 1

current_progress.text: Visual layout control enhances robustness under tight memory budgets by enabling non-uniform budget allocation.
current_progress.key_result: Removing visual layout from the proposed method causes marked performance degradation, especially at low budgets. At 16 tokens, the proposed method achieves comparable accuracy to textual baselines at 64 tokens, demonstrating an 8× token-efficiency gain. The ablation without visual layout shows substantially larger performance drops as budgets tighten, confirming that robustness comes primarily from layout-guided allocation rather than the visual modality alone.
current_progress.citation: Section 4.3 (Analysis on Visual Robustness - RQ2), Figure 3

current_progress.text: Different layout regions exhibit asymmetric compression-robustness, and the proposed method exploits this by placing important evidence in more visually accessible regions.
current_progress.key_result: Oracle experiments show that injecting ground-truth evidence into the crucial region (H1 headers) consistently outperforms injection into the detailed region (plain body text), with advantages growing as budgets tighten. Training analysis reveals the proposed method shifts evidence density: crucial region precision increases ~1.8× while detailed region precision decreases ~0.46×, while the crucial region remains orders of magnitude shorter, demonstrating adaptive information density.
current_progress.citation: Section 4.4 (Mechanism Verification - RQ3), Figures 4 and 5

current_progress.text: Budget-aware training objectives with multiple complementary tasks are necessary for learning robust layout-based memory allocation.
current_progress.key_result: Progressive ablation shows cumulative robustness gains: removing all training objectives yields 37.5% average accuracy at 16 tokens (10K context); using only standard QA achieves 32.9%; adding detail-oriented questions improves to 41.2%; the full model with compressed memory training reaches 62.2%. The low-budget performance drop increases from 16.6% (full model) to 47.1% (standard QA only), confirming that multiple training signals are essential for budget-robust behavior.
current_progress.citation: Section 4.5 (Ablation Study over Training Objectives - RQ4), Table 2

rephrased_option_1: Improve the budget allocation policy by introducing more flexible structured text formats.
rephrased_option_2: Incorporate multi-objective optimization techniques to enable more effective exploration of the efficiency-performance trade-off.

research_goal: To develop and demonstrate a test-time training method that enables language models to discover new state-of-the-art solutions to scientific and engineering problems by continuing to learn on individual test instances, rather than relying solely on frozen models and search heuristics.

current_progress.text: Test-time training (TTT) with an entropic objective and PUCT-based reuse can discover new state-of-the-art solutions across diverse scientific problems with continuous rewards, outperforming both frozen-LLM search methods and standard RL approaches.
current_progress.key_result: The proposed method achieved new state-of-the-art results in almost all attempted problems: (i) a mathematical optimization problem (0.380876 vs. 0.380924 previous best), an autocorrelation inequality (1.50287 vs. 1.50314 previous best); (ii) a community GPU-kernel optimization competition (up to 2× faster than the best human on a high-end GPU); (iii) competitive-programming contests (567,062 vs. 566,997 best human on one contest, 848,414,228 vs. 847,674,723 best human on another contest); (iv) single-cell denoising (0.71 vs. 0.64 best human). All results achieved with a 120B-parameter open-source model, while previous AI results used closed frontier models.
current_progress.citation: Abstract, Section 1 (Introduction), Section 4 (Applications)

current_progress.text: The entropic objective J_β(θ) = E[log E[e^(βR)]] with adaptive β better optimizes for discovery (finding one exceptional solution) compared to standard RL objectives that optimize expected reward, because it strongly favors maximum-reward actions while maintaining training stability.
current_progress.key_result: Ablation studies on the GPU-kernel optimization task showed: TTT with adaptive entropic objective achieved 1203.10 μs, significantly outperforming TTT with constant β=2 (1483.83 μs), TTT with expected reward objective (1985.67 μs), and naive RL baseline (5328.73 μs). The adaptive β formulation using KL constraint (γ=ln(2)) allowed the method to automatically adjust exploration-exploitation trade-off based on the difficulty of improvements at each state.
current_progress.citation: Section 3.1 (Naive RL at Test Time), Section 3.2 (TTT-Discover), Section 4.6 (Ablations), Table 4, Figure 4

current_progress.text: PUCT-based state reuse that prioritizes states by Q(s) + exploration bonus (where Q uses maximum child reward rather than mean) enables more effective exploration and exploitation compared to simpler reuse strategies or no reuse, because it balances exploiting promising states while maintaining diversity.
current_progress.key_result: Ablation studies showed: PUCT reuse with TTT achieved 1203.10 μs on the GPU-kernel optimization task, outperforming ε-greedy reuse (1328.89 μs) and no reuse (5274.03 μs). The use of maximum child reward in Q(s) rather than mean reward was critical for discovery tasks where the goal is finding one exceptional solution rather than robust average performance.
current_progress.citation: Section 3.1 (Naive RL at Test Time), Section 3.2 (TTT-Discover), Section 4.6 (Ablations), Appendix A.2 (PUCT Prioritization)

current_progress.text: Continuing to train the LLM policy at test time on experience specific to a single problem enables discovering solutions that require ideas beyond the model's training data, whereas frozen-LLM search methods (like evolutionary algorithms) cannot internalize new problem-specific knowledge.
current_progress.key_result: Across all applications, the proposed method consistently outperformed Best-of-25600 baseline (same sampling budget, frozen model): mathematics (0.380876 vs. 0.380906), kernel engineering (1161.2 μs vs. 5390.3 μs), algorithms (567,062 vs. 554,171), and biology (denoising: 0.71 vs. 0.62). The reward distributions show the proposed method progressively generates better solutions over training steps, while Best-of-N remains static.
current_progress.citation: Section 1 (Introduction), Section 3 (Learning to Discover at Test Time), Section 4 (Applications), Figure 1, Tables 2-6

rephrased_option_1: Extend test-time training methods to handle problems with sparse or binary rewards and to non-verifiable domains, beyond the current limitation to continuous reward settings.
rephrased_option_2: Extend explicit planning mechanisms to handle stochastic or partially observable settings by integrating lookahead with belief-state inference, uncertainty-aware evaluation, or learned world models.

research_goal: To develop and demonstrate an environment for training reinforcement learning-based search agents to answer scientific questions by searching and reasoning over biomedical research papers, including creating the necessary datasets, retrieval corpus, and benchmarks to enable this capability.

current_progress.text: RLVR training of search agents leads to stronger performance on scientific paper question-answering compared to non-RL baselines including direct LLM inference, chain-of-thought prompting, retrieval augmented generation (RAG), an agentic search-with-reasoning method, and a pre-packaged RAG pipeline for academic papers.
current_progress.key_result: RLVR training using an established search-training framework achieved the strongest results across all baselines. For 3B models, RL improved over RAG by 9.6 and 5.5 points for the proposed dataset and a standard biomedical QA benchmark respectively. For 7B models, the difference was 14.5 and 9.3 points. On the proposed dataset, the RLVR agent achieved 41.6% (3B) and 51.0% (7B) accuracy compared to RAG's 32.0% (3B) and 36.5% (7B).
current_progress.citation: Section 4 (Results), specifically Section 4.2 (Quantitative Results) and Table 1

current_progress.text: Factoid question-answer pairs with verifiable answers can serve as effective training data for reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) methods for scientific literature search agents.
current_progress.key_result: The automatically generated proposed dataset with 54,907 training samples of factoid QAs successfully enabled RLVR training that outperformed all baseline methods. The dataset's design with unambiguous factoid answers allowed for simple exact-match reward computation (binary 0/1), which proved sufficient for effective RL training as demonstrated by the 9.6-14.5 point improvements over RAG baselines.
current_progress.citation: Section 3.1 (Dataset Construction) and Section 4.2 (Quantitative Results)

current_progress.text: Search agents trained with RLVR on scientific papers will exhibit emergent behaviors including explicit planning about query rewriting, reasoning about questions before retrieval, and verification when the model already has an initial answer.
current_progress.key_result: Manual review of reasoning traces revealed three prevalent patterns: (1) explicit planning and keyword extraction was very common in later training, where models extracted keywords and combined them into search queries; (2) reasoning before search, where LLMs reasoned about questions using parametric knowledge before performing search; (3) verification of in-parameter knowledge, where models performed search to verify initial answers they already knew. Notably, behavior 1 becomes much more common with more training.
current_progress.citation: Section 4.3 (Qualitative Results) and Figure 3

current_progress.text: Paraphrasing questions during dataset construction to avoid exact keyword matching from source documents increases dataset difficulty and is beneficial for training robust search agents.
current_progress.key_result: For the RLVR agent trained on the proposed dataset, non-paraphrased questions scored 57.2% while paraphrased questions scored 44.9%, demonstrating a 12.3 point difference. This highlights the importance of paraphrasing for sustaining question difficulty and preventing agents from relying on simple keyword matching.
current_progress.citation: Section 4.2 (Quantitative Results), subsection "Paraphrasing in data construction is beneficial"

rephrased_option_1: Apply mitigations to the data generation methodology by excluding problematic question categories identified through human review or by designing a workflow to retrieve all relevant papers to check for conflicts.
rephrased_option_2: Train a more agentic model capable of planning, deciding when to invoke tools, and coordinating tool use more effectively to enable better credit assignment and more efficient inference-time computation.

research_goal: The overarching research goal is to develop a scalable and reliable paradigm for training autonomous GUI agents by shifting from passive, post-hoc verification to proactive, in-situ self-verification. Specifically, the work aims to create self-verifying agents that simultaneously learn to complete complex tasks and curate minimal, decisive snapshot evidence to prove their accomplishment, thereby reducing verification costs and improving reliability in agentic reinforcement learning.

current_progress.text: The self-verifying agent paradigm, which shifts verification from passive post-hoc analysis to proactive in-situ self-verification by the agent itself, enables scalable and reliable training of LLM-driven agents for GUI tasks.
current_progress.key_result: The proposed system achieves performance gains up to 26.08% and 16.66% respectively for 8B and 30B models across models spanning 7B-32B parameters from two open-source families on a standard Android agent benchmark. The trained agents (e.g., the proposed system with the 8B and 32B models from the same family) achieve competitive performance with much larger frontier models, demonstrating that the self-verification paradigm fundamentally enhances training efficiency and scalability.
current_progress.citation: Section 4.3 (Main Results), Table 2, Figure 1

current_progress.text: The three principles (completeness, conciseness, and creativity) for evidence curation create a synergistic learning loop where the requirement to find and present evidence compels the agent to develop deeper understanding of tasks, as the knowledge required for successful execution and effective proof are deeply intertwined.
current_progress.key_result: Fine-tuning with self-verification (following the completeness, conciseness, creativity principle) substantially outperforms vanilla fine-tuning using a standard Android instruction-tuning dataset across all models. For example, a 7B model achieves 30.15% SR with the proposed FT versus 20.28% with vanilla FT (+17.83% gain), and an 8B model achieves 26.81% versus 19.56% (+16.66% gain). The qualitative analysis shows agents learn to decompose tasks into actionable checklists and proactively create evidence-oriented actions (e.g., taking snapshots that list target transactions), demonstrating the synergy between task completion and evidence curation.
current_progress.citation: Section 4.3 (Main Results), Table 2; Section 4.4 (Qualitative Analysis, Case Study), Figures 7-8

current_progress.text: Grounding evidence in objective, unalterable tool interactions (action-observation pairs) rather than agent self-declarations prevents reward hacking and enables trustworthy verification, as agents cannot fabricate or manipulate the evidence.
current_progress.key_result: The RL training dynamics show consistent improvement in training reward with decreasing intra-group variance, indicating agents learn to complete tasks and curate valid evidence without gaming the system. The number of evidences converges toward 1.5 on average (satisfying the conciseness principle), and agents learn more compact interaction patterns (decreasing number of agent turns and response length over training). The evidence validity check shows agents successfully navigate toward relevant application screens even when tasks ultimately fail, demonstrating the robustness of the grounded evidence formulation.
current_progress.citation: Section 4.4 (Qualitative Analysis - Behavior Evolution and Performance Variation), Figures 5-6

current_progress.text: Intrinsic reward shaping through structured, dense feedback from an LLM/VLM verifier (including evidence validity, strict evidence-to-task grounding, format compliance, and conciseness penalties) accelerates the agent's acquisition of evidence curation skills during reinforcement learning.
current_progress.key_result: RL with the proposed system's reward shaping consistently improves performance beyond SFT across all model families. For instance, one 8B model improves from 23.91% (FT) to 31.15% (RL). Across three models from a different family, the 7B model improves from 30.15% to 30.43%, the 8B model from 26.81% to 36.23%, and the 32B model from 28.98% to 34.78%. The training reward curves show monotonic improvement with decreasing variance, and agents develop efficient behaviors (fewer turns, shorter responses, minimal evidence sets) that satisfy the reward shaping objectives.
current_progress.citation: Section 3.4 (Evidence Verification and Reward Shaping), Section 4.3 (Main Results), Table 2; Section 4.4 (Qualitative Analysis), Figures 5-6

rephrased_option_1: Collect domain-specific training corpora and conduct comprehensive continued pre-training and supervised fine-tuning to prepare foundation models for reinforcement learning on specialized task domains.
rephrased_option_2: Investigate methods for creating evaluation criteria that are both rigorous and deterministic while avoiding overly specific or prescriptive constraints.

research_goal: The overarching research goal is to develop and validate a large-scale, efficient training environment that enables visual web agents to learn generalizable skills for solving diverse, realistic web navigation tasks through reinforcement learning. This encompasses three interconnected objectives: (1) constructing a sufficiently large and diverse task set with structured evaluation criteria that spans multiple domains and difficulty levels, (2) building a high-throughput asynchronous rollout system that makes large-scale RL training computationally feasible, and (3) demonstrating that simple RL algorithms can leverage this environment to train agents that significantly outperform existing approaches, including proprietary models, on entirely unseen websites.

current_progress.text: Domain breadth (diversity across websites and subdomains) improves agent generalization to unseen websites, even when the difficulty mixing ratio is unchanged.
current_progress.key_result: Removing half of the subdomains from the training set ("exclude domains") consistently slowed down improvements and lowered the final success rate across all evaluation slices (from 34.5% to 31.0%), despite keeping the same difficulty mixing ratio. This gap persisted even though the number of collected training trajectories (36k) was far smaller than the number of remaining training tasks (~150k), indicating the degradation reflects reduced domain diversity rather than oversampling.
current_progress.citation: Section 5.3, "Scaling task set breadth (domain coverage) for better generalization"

current_progress.text: Scaling the candidate task set size by sampling more easy tasks (which are more diverse) avoids overfitting to narrow hard tasks when using filtered behavior cloning loss.
current_progress.key_result: Training exclusively on easy tasks ("only easy") consistently outperformed "only medium" on overall success and on easy/medium test tasks, and did not exhibit the late-stage plateauing or performance regression observed when harder tasks were upweighted (e.g., "biased to hard" after ~36k trajectories). The "only easy" setting achieved 36.9% success rate compared to 34.5% for "biased to hard", demonstrating that the large set of easy tasks spanning many websites and domains supports generalization and mitigates overfitting.
current_progress.citation: Section 5.3, "Scaling candidate task set size by sampling more easy tasks avoids overfitting to narrow hard tasks with the filtered BC loss"

current_progress.text: Scaling candidate task set depth (wider range of difficulty levels) by including tasks from all difficulty levels promotes more efficient learning, particularly on medium-difficulty tasks.
current_progress.key_result: Uniform sampling from the full training set yielded the highest overall performance (38.2%) among the four difficulty-mixing strategies evaluated, and achieved a significantly higher success rate on the medium-difficulty test set compared to training on easy tasks alone (36.9%). The additional gains from uniform sampling over "only easy" are attributed to the inclusion of medium- and hard-difficulty tasks, which provide training signals enabling the agent to develop capabilities beyond easy interactions.
current_progress.citation: Section 5.3, "Scaling candidate task set depth (wider range of difficulty levels) by including tasks from all difficulty levels to promote more efficient learning"

current_progress.text: Shortening the train-time interaction horizon (step budget) acts as a regularizer that improves both sample efficiency and final performance by concentrating training signal on earlier, higher-impact decisions.
current_progress.key_result: Reducing the per-episode step limit from (15, 30, 45) to (10, 20, 30) improved both sample efficiency and final performance across all difficulty slices. The success rate increased from a peak of 38.2% (uniform sampling with step budget of (15, 30, 45)) to a peak of 42.9%. Performance on hard tasks also improved substantially, consistent with the model learning more robust and reusable interaction primitives that transfer beyond the capped training horizon.
current_progress.citation: Section 5.3, "Horizon: controlling train-time interaction budget"

current_progress.text: An explicit memory mechanism is essential for enabling agents to retain and reuse information gathered in earlier steps for long-horizon tasks.
current_progress.key_result: Utilizing the memory prompt (where the model maintains and updates memory by responding with updated memory in each step) significantly boosted performance after RL training. The agent with memory prompt achieved 34.5% success rate compared to 31.5% without the memory prompt ("w/o memory prompt"), as shown in the ablation results.
current_progress.citation: Section 5.2, "Memory prompt for long-horizon tasks" and Table in Appendix A (raw results)

current_progress.text: Applying a penalty for repeated ineffective actions improves sample efficiency during RL training by reducing the frequency of agents getting stuck repeating the same action.
current_progress.key_result: Applying the repetition penalty (filtering out steps where the next screenshot is identical to the current one) made RL runs substantially more sample efficient. The agent with repetition penalty achieved 34.5% success rate compared to 33.0% without it ("w/o repetition penalty"). The penalty also reduced the "same screenshot" rate and improved trajectory efficiency.
current_progress.citation: Section 5.2, "Removing repeated actions with a reward penalty" and Table in Appendix A (raw results)

current_progress.text: Rubric-based evaluation (with explicit task-specific criteria) improves agreement with human judgment compared to task-only evaluation.
current_progress.key_result: Rubric-guided evaluation improved agreement over task-only judging, increasing accuracy and precision for all evaluator models tested (three evaluator models — a closed frontier model, an 8B open-source vision-language model, and a 27B open-source model). For example, the closed frontier model showed the largest improvement: precision increased from approximately 0.70 to 0.85 when using rubrics, though recall dropped slightly from approximately 0.85 to 0.75.
current_progress.citation: Section 3.5, "Evaluator and Reward Function Design" and Figure 2

current_progress.text: The asynchronous rollout system in the proposed environment achieves 4-5× speedup compared to synchronous rollout frameworks by eliminating burst-idle behavior and keeping both CPUs and GPUs fully utilized.
current_progress.key_result: Using only 64 CPUs, the asynchronous system collected 1.8k rollouts (with average 13.2 steps, totaling 23,760 steps) in 48.6 minutes, whereas the synchronous system required 264 minutes—demonstrating a 5.4× speedup. The speedup was 4-5× across different CPU configurations, with greater gains under tighter CPU constraints.
current_progress.citation: Section 4.2, "Benchmarking our Rollout System" and Figure 4 (left panel)

rephrased_option_1: Develop evaluation methods that treat automatically generated assessment criteria as soft guidance by training evaluators that jointly condition on both the criteria and task descriptions, rather than applying them as strict binary rules.
rephrased_option_2: Incorporate human feedback mechanisms to refine automatically generated evaluation criteria through review, template reuse, and failure-mode-specific guidance.

research_goal: The overarching research goal of this project is to develop an efficient and adaptive video understanding framework that selectively applies chain-of-thought reasoning only when necessary, rather than unconditionally for all inputs. Specifically, this project aims to create a system that can automatically determine when explicit step-by-step reasoning provides genuine benefits for video understanding tasks versus when direct answering is sufficient, thereby achieving both high accuracy on challenging reasoning-intensive problems and improved computational efficiency on perception-oriented tasks.

current_progress.text: For RL-trained video models, direct answering often matches or even surpasses CoT performance, despite CoT producing step-by-step analyses at a higher computational cost.
current_progress.key_result: Analysis of three existing video reasoning models (covering general reasoning, temporal grounding, and spatio-temporal perception) showed that direct inference often matches or outperforms CoT inference on perception-oriented benchmarks for general and long video understanding, while generating significantly fewer tokens (e.g., 17.6 vs 386 tokens for the general-reasoning video model). Consistent CoT gains were primarily observed only on reasoning-intensive benchmarks.
current_progress.citation: Section 3.2 (Preliminaries - Analysis of Existing Video Reasoning Models), Table 1

current_progress.text: A think-aware dual-answer training paradigm that supervises both initial and reviewed answers eliminates the need for per-sample think/no-think labels and yields more stable training than traditional mode-switching approaches.
current_progress.key_result: The proposed dual-answer training approach achieved superior performance compared to training-based mode-switching strategies. The proposed framework consistently outperformed the training-based variant across benchmarks (e.g., 58.6% vs 55.7%) and avoided mode collapse issues, while the training-based approach showed unstable think ratios (e.g., only 1% on one benchmark vs 31% on another).
current_progress.citation: Section 4.1 (Method - Thinking Once, Answering Twice), Section 5.3 (Experiments - Comparison with Other Adaptive Reasoning Strategies), Table 7

current_progress.text: A confidence-based early-exit mechanism using length-normalized mean log probability of the initial answer can effectively determine when to invoke CoT reasoning, with the confidence score correlating with mode-switch accuracy.
current_progress.key_result: The confidence score of the initial answer strongly correlated with task difficulty: perception-oriented benchmarks showed high average confidence (>93%) with low think ratios (~25-39%), while a reasoning-intensive benchmark showed lower confidence (87%) with higher think ratio (51%). The mechanism achieved 94-100% recall on samples marked think-compulsory, i.e. where the initial answer was wrong but the reviewed answer was correct.
current_progress.citation: Section 4.3 (Method - Inference: Confidence-Based Early Exit), Section 5.3 (Experiments - Analysis of Confidence-Based Early Exit Mechanism), Table 8

current_progress.text: The proposed framework achieves state-of-the-art accuracy with significantly improved efficiency by reasoning only when necessary, reducing average response length while maintaining or improving performance.
current_progress.key_result: The proposed framework achieved state-of-the-art results across benchmarks while reducing average response length from ~149 tokens (RL with thinking baseline) to 44 tokens. On a reasoning-intensive benchmark, it improved accuracy from 54.7% to 58.6% (+3.9%) compared to a VLM baseline, and on a perception-oriented benchmark from 66.0% to 67.3% (+1.3%). The model adaptively activated thinking mode based on task complexity: 25% on a perception-oriented benchmark vs 51% on a reasoning-heavy benchmark.
current_progress.citation: Section 5.2 (Experiments - Main Results), Tables 2-4, Abstract

current_progress.text: Assigning higher weight to the reviewed answer (w₂ > w₁) in the dual-answer reward and including a fallback tolerance mechanism improves model performance compared to equal weighting.
current_progress.key_result: Asymmetric reward weighting (0.9:1.1 for w₁:w₂) with fallback bonus (α=0.3) achieved the best performance across three benchmarks: 67.3%, 58.6%, and 60.0 mIoU. This outperformed equal weighting (1:1) which achieved 66.1%, 56.1%, and 58.3% respectively. The fallback reward consistently improved results across all tested weight ratios.
current_progress.citation: Section 5.3 (Experiments - Ablation Study of Dual-Answer Reward Design), Table 9, Appendix Section on Reward Analysis

current_progress.text: Direct RL training without cold-start SFT on chain-of-thought data is more effective than SFT followed by RL for video reasoning tasks.
current_progress.key_result: Direct RL training achieved 66.1%, 71.2%, and 56.4% across three benchmarks. In contrast, SFT on the general-reasoning model's CoT data degraded the baseline (60.1%, 64.0%, 53.8% respectively), and SFT→RL performed worse than direct RL (61.7%, 64.3%, 53.5%). This demonstrated that low-quality CoT supervision can distort strong base models and create poor initialization for RL.
current_progress.citation: Appendix Section A.5 (Analysis of the Impact of Cold-Start SFT), Table 12

rephrased_option_1: Develop large-scale video datasets that explicitly require complex reasoning capabilities such as multi-step causal inference, temporal logic problems, or physically grounded scenarios.
rephrased_option_2: Validate the findings by conducting experiments across diverse model architectures, sampling strategies, scale variations, and training paradigms.

research_goal: To develop and validate an autonomous, minimally supervised agentic AI framework that can detect supply chain disruptions from unstructured sources, map them to extended multi-tier supplier networks, assess cascading risks, and generate actionable mitigation strategies—thereby enabling organizations to shift from reactive recovery to proactive disruption management across deep-tier supply chains where most disruptions originate but visibility is typically absent.

current_progress.text: Agentic AI, defined as agents driven by large language models (LLMs) that plan, use tools, and coordinate via language, can autonomously perform end-to-end supply chain disruption monitoring by detecting disruptions from unstructured sources, mapping them to extended supplier networks across multiple tiers, tracing propagation paths toward Tier-1, quantifying risk based on network structure, and deriving actionable decisions.
current_progress.key_result: The framework achieves high accuracy across core tasks with F1 scores between 0.962 and 0.991 across four agents (disruption monitoring: 0.991±0.028, knowledge graph query: 0.980±0.110, risk manager: 0.962±0.196, chief supply chain officer: 0.899±0.277), and performs full end-to-end analyses in a mean of 3.83 minutes at a cost of $0.0836 per disruption across 30 synthesized scenarios. This represents a reduction of more than three orders of magnitude in response time compared to industry benchmarks of multi-day, analyst-driven assessments (five days on average).
current_progress.citation: Section 4 (Experimental Evaluation), specifically Section 4.3 (Results and Discussion) and Table 1 (Overall Performance Metrics)

current_progress.text: A minimally supervised agentic framework combining LLM-powered agents with deterministic graph tools and structured communication protocols can address the fundamental gap in supply chain disruption monitoring: transforming unstructured disruption signals (news articles, regulatory filings) into mapped network knowledge and actionable intelligence across extended multi-tier supplier networks where over one-third of disruptions originate beyond Tier-1 suppliers.
current_progress.key_result: The framework successfully processes unstructured news articles to identify disrupted supply chain paths across all four tiers (Tier-1 through Tier-4) with perfect precision (1.000±0.000) for the knowledge graph query agent and high recall (0.975±0.137). A real-world case study based on a recent global conflict demonstrates operational applicability, identifying critical dependencies that exist at Tier-2 but affect Tier-1 suppliers, enabling proactive mitigation before disruptions cascade.
current_progress.citation: Section 4.3 (Results and Discussion), specifically subsections 4.3.1 (Disruption Monitoring Agent) and 4.3.2 (Knowledge Graph Query Agent); Section 5 (Case Study: Russia-Ukraine War), specifically subsections 5.1-5.2

current_progress.text: Grounding critical computations in deterministic tools while using LLM orchestration for reasoning and tool selection creates a hybrid architecture that ensures reliability and reproducibility for supply chain risk assessment while maintaining flexibility to handle diverse disruption scenarios.
current_progress.key_result: The risk manager agent achieves perfect precision (1.000±0.000) through orchestration of deterministic risk calculation tools that aggregate four risk dimensions (exposure depth, exposure breadth, downstream criticality, network centrality) using fixed mathematical formulas. The knowledge graph query agent similarly achieves perfect precision (1.000±0.000) through deterministic graph-database queries. Error analysis reveals that when upstream agents provide correct inputs, downstream agents achieve high performance, demonstrating that the architecture successfully isolates errors to the initial extraction stage and prevents error amplification through deterministic tool grounding.
current_progress.citation: Section 4.3 (Results and Discussion), specifically subsections 4.3.2 (Knowledge Graph Query Agent), 4.3.3 (Risk Manager Agent), and 4.3.5 (Error Propagation and Framework Robustness)

current_progress.text: The framework can generate executive-level strategic decisions and qualitative analysis that supports decision-making with clear reasoning, providing comprehensive, accurate, and actionable supply chain intelligence beyond quantitative risk scores.
current_progress.key_result: The chief supply chain officer agent achieves strong quantitative performance (F1: 0.899±0.277, Precision: 0.950±0.201) in addressing the top 10 riskiest suppliers with appropriate actions. Qualitative evaluation using a rubric-based methodology shows weighted mean scores of 0.811±0.049 for disruption summary, 0.486±0.172 for network impact analysis, and 0.830±0.078 for replacement recommendations, with particularly high actionability (0.910±0.111), indicating the agent provides clear, actionable guidance aligned with risk assessments. The overall qualitative score of 0.709±0.099 demonstrates executive-level communication quality.
current_progress.citation: Section 4.3.4 (CSCO Agent: Strategic Decision-Making) and Section 4.3.4.1 (Qualitative Evaluation of CSCO Agent), including Table 4 (Qualitative Evaluation Results)

rephrased_option_1: Enhance entity extraction robustness through ensemble methods or confidence-based filtering approaches.
rephrased_option_2: Explore integrating agentic reinforcement learning to jointly optimize the tool-use policy.

research_goal: To develop and evaluate an automated system that can generate, implement, and execute research ideas for open-ended AI research problems, and to investigate whether large language models can learn to generate more effective ideas by grounding their ideation process in execution feedback through evolutionary search and reinforcement learning.

current_progress.text: Frontier LLMs can generate ideas that outperform provided baselines through best-of-N sampling, and a large fraction of LLM-generated ideas can be successfully executed automatically on open-ended AI research problems.
current_progress.key_result: Benchmarking experiments showed that frontier LLMs achieved high execution rates (over 90% for two frontier closed-source models on a popular pre-training task with self-execution), and best-of-N performance (N=50) beat baseline solutions. For example, one frontier closed-source model achieved 60.4% max accuracy vs 48.0% baseline on a conventional post-training task; another achieved 3.237 min loss vs 3.255 baseline on the pre-training task.
current_progress.citation: Section 3: Benchmarking LLM Ideators and Executors; Figure 2

current_progress.text: Execution-guided evolutionary search can find research ideas that significantly outperform baseline solutions on realistic LLM research problems (pre-training and post-training) within a limited number of search epochs.
current_progress.key_result: Within 10 search epochs, execution-guided search found a post-training recipe achieving 69.4% vs 48.0% baseline on the post-training task, and a pre-training recipe achieving 19.7 minutes vs 35.9 minutes baseline on the pre-training task to reach target validation loss. The post-training result outperformed the best human expert solution (68.8%) from a public class leaderboard.
current_progress.citation: Section 4: Execution-Guided Evolutionary Search; Table 1; Figure 3

current_progress.text: Execution-guided evolutionary search is more sample-efficient than best-of-N sampling under the same sampling budget.
current_progress.key_result: Comparison on the pre-training task with a frontier closed-source model showed that evolutionary search significantly outperformed best-of-N from epoch 1 onward under the same sampling budget (N=80 ideas per epoch), demonstrating that the model effectively leverages trajectories from previous epochs to generate more effective ideas.
current_progress.citation: Section 4.3: Comparison with Best-of-N; Figure 4

current_progress.text: LLMs generate substantial amounts of algorithmic ideas (beyond hyperparameter tuning) during execution-guided search, and algorithmic ideas achieve higher upper-bound performance than hyperparameter ideas in most cases.
current_progress.key_result: Quantitative analysis showed three frontier closed-source models generated substantial algorithmic ideas: 96.3%, 58.9%, and 95.0% respectively on the post-training task; 91.3%, 68.7%, and 84.6% on the pre-training task. Algorithmic ideas achieved the best performance for all models except the second.
current_progress.citation: Section 4.4: Analysis of Generated Ideas; Table 2

current_progress.text: Reinforcement learning from execution reward can improve the average reward of ideator models on open-ended AI research problems.
current_progress.key_result: RL training with GRPO on a 30B open-source model successfully improved average reward: on the post-training task, average accuracy increased from 0.253 to 0.343 after 40 epochs; on the pre-training task, average reward increased from 0.194 to 0.246 after 68 epochs (corresponding to average validation loss decreasing from 5.150 to 4.066).
current_progress.citation: Section 5: Reinforcement Learning from Execution Reward; Section 5.2: Experiment Results; Figure 5 (upper row)

current_progress.text: Reinforcement learning from execution reward improves the maximum reward (upper-bound performance) of ideator models on open-ended AI research problems.
current_progress.key_result: This hypothesis was invalidated. The max reward fluctuated throughout RL training without a clear upward trend on both the post-training and pre-training tasks, revealing a crucial limitation of the standard GRPO algorithm for improving idea generation where upper-bound performance matters more than average quality.
current_progress.citation: Section 5.2: Experiment Results, paragraph "The Case of Max Reward"; Figure 5 (lower row)

current_progress.text: RL from execution reward causes models to converge on simple, easy-to-implement ideas, leading to diversity collapse and shrinking thinking lengths.
current_progress.key_result: Analysis confirmed this hypothesis: (1) Thinking trace lengths rapidly decreased during RL training while idea lengths stayed constant; (2) Ideas with longer thinking had consistently lower execution rates; (3) Severe diversity collapse occurred - in the pre-training task, 119 out of 128 sampled ideas at epoch 68 were one of two common simple ideas (replacing RMSNorm with LayerNorm or performing EMA), compared to 51 out of 128 at epoch 0.
current_progress.citation: Section 5.3: Analysis of Training Dynamics; Figures 6 and 7

rephrased_option_1: Improve the execution agent to enable correct implementation of more complex idea types, such as those requiring additional model training or system-level optimizations.
rephrased_option_2: Investigate designed verification tasks, more principled integration of verification and generation objectives, and more efficient training strategies to further leverage verification's potential to improve generation.

research_goal: To develop and demonstrate an environment for training reinforcement learning-based search agents to answer scientific questions by searching and reasoning over biomedical research papers, including creating the necessary datasets, retrieval corpus, and benchmarks to enable this capability.

current_progress.text: RLVR training of search agents leads to stronger performance on scientific paper question-answering compared to non-RL baselines including direct LLM inference, chain-of-thought prompting, retrieval augmented generation (RAG), an agentic search-with-reasoning method, and a pre-packaged RAG pipeline for academic papers.
current_progress.key_result: RLVR training using an established search-training framework achieved the strongest results across all baselines. For 3B models, RL improved over RAG by 9.6 and 5.5 points for the proposed dataset and a standard biomedical QA benchmark respectively. For 7B models, the difference was 14.5 and 9.3 points. On the proposed dataset, the RLVR agent achieved 41.6% (3B) and 51.0% (7B) accuracy compared to RAG's 32.0% (3B) and 36.5% (7B).
current_progress.citation: Section 4 (Results), specifically Section 4.2 (Quantitative Results) and Table 1

current_progress.text: Factoid question-answer pairs with verifiable answers can serve as effective training data for reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) methods for scientific literature search agents.
current_progress.key_result: The automatically generated proposed dataset with 54,907 training samples of factoid QAs successfully enabled RLVR training that outperformed all baseline methods. The dataset's design with unambiguous factoid answers allowed for simple exact-match reward computation (binary 0/1), which proved sufficient for effective RL training as demonstrated by the 9.6-14.5 point improvements over RAG baselines.
current_progress.citation: Section 3.1 (Dataset Construction) and Section 4.2 (Quantitative Results)

current_progress.text: Search agents trained with RLVR on scientific papers will exhibit emergent behaviors including explicit planning about query rewriting, reasoning about questions before retrieval, and verification when the model already has an initial answer.
current_progress.key_result: Manual review of reasoning traces revealed three prevalent patterns: (1) explicit planning and keyword extraction was very common in later training, where models extracted keywords and combined them into search queries; (2) reasoning before search, where LLMs reasoned about questions using parametric knowledge before performing search; (3) verification of in-parameter knowledge, where models performed search to verify initial answers they already knew. Notably, behavior 1 becomes much more common with more training.
current_progress.citation: Section 4.3 (Qualitative Results) and Figure 3

current_progress.text: Paraphrasing questions during dataset construction to avoid exact keyword matching from source documents increases dataset difficulty and is beneficial for training robust search agents.
current_progress.key_result: For the RLVR agent trained on the proposed dataset, non-paraphrased questions scored 57.2% while paraphrased questions scored 44.9%, demonstrating a 12.3 point difference. This highlights the importance of paraphrasing for sustaining question difficulty and preventing agents from relying on simple keyword matching.
current_progress.citation: Section 4.2 (Quantitative Results), subsection "Paraphrasing in data construction is beneficial"

rephrased_option_1: Equip agents with tools and metadata that real scientists use in their work, such as citation traversal capabilities and source reliability metrics.
rephrased_option_2: Incorporate human-in-the-loop capabilities that enable subject-matter experts to approve, revise, or redirect agent decisions to increase system reliability and alignment with real-world scientific practices.

research_goal: To develop a memory management system for long-horizon agentic reasoning that achieves adaptive information density through visual layout, enabling more efficient utilization of limited context budgets by decoupling semantic content from token cost.

current_progress.text: Visual memory with adaptive information density improves long-horizon reasoning under tight context budgets compared to textual memory paradigms.
current_progress.key_result: The proposed method outperforms text-based baselines across all settings, achieving 74.6% average accuracy vs. 67.8% for the strongest agentic memory baseline at 1024 tokens on 10K context. Under extreme budgets (16 tokens), the proposed method maintains 62.2% accuracy while the strongest agentic memory baseline drops to 31.6%, representing a 30.6 percentage point improvement and approximately 8× improvement in effective context utilization.
current_progress.citation: Section 4.2 (Overall Performance - RQ1), Table 1

current_progress.text: Visual layout control enhances robustness under tight memory budgets by enabling non-uniform budget allocation.
current_progress.key_result: Removing visual layout from the proposed method causes marked performance degradation, especially at low budgets. At 16 tokens, the proposed method achieves comparable accuracy to textual baselines at 64 tokens, demonstrating an 8× token-efficiency gain. The ablation without visual layout shows substantially larger performance drops as budgets tighten, confirming that robustness comes primarily from layout-guided allocation rather than the visual modality alone.
current_progress.citation: Section 4.3 (Analysis on Visual Robustness - RQ2), Figure 3

current_progress.text: Different layout regions exhibit asymmetric compression-robustness, and the proposed method exploits this by placing important evidence in more visually accessible regions.
current_progress.key_result: Oracle experiments show that injecting ground-truth evidence into the crucial region (H1 headers) consistently outperforms injection into the detailed region (plain body text), with advantages growing as budgets tighten. Training analysis reveals the proposed method shifts evidence density: crucial region precision increases ~1.8× while detailed region precision decreases ~0.46×, while the crucial region remains orders of magnitude shorter, demonstrating adaptive information density.
current_progress.citation: Section 4.4 (Mechanism Verification - RQ3), Figures 4 and 5

current_progress.text: Budget-aware training objectives with multiple complementary tasks are necessary for learning robust layout-based memory allocation.
current_progress.key_result: Progressive ablation shows cumulative robustness gains: removing all training objectives yields 37.5% average accuracy at 16 tokens (10K context); using only standard QA achieves 32.9%; adding detail-oriented questions improves to 41.2%; the full model with compressed memory training reaches 62.2%. The low-budget performance drop increases from 16.6% (full model) to 47.1% (standard QA only), confirming that multiple training signals are essential for budget-robust behavior.
current_progress.citation: Section 4.5 (Ablation Study over Training Objectives - RQ4), Table 2

rephrased_option_1: Extend the visual memory approach to additional long-horizon agent tasks beyond question answering, such as planning and tool-augmented reasoning, and investigate its long-term stability under continuous lifelong updates.
rephrased_option_2: Investigate question-answering tasks that require multi-hop reasoning, list-based answers, and extended or summarized responses, which necessitate more complex agent planning behaviors and fuzzy reward models.

research_goal: To develop a comprehensive multi-turn interactive reinforcement learning framework for autonomous driving trajectory planning that enables vision-language models to iteratively refine trajectories based on environmental feedback, addressing the limitations of single-turn reasoning in complex driving scenarios while achieving efficient training through system-level optimizations.

current_progress.text: Multi-turn interactive reasoning can improve trajectory planning quality in autonomous driving by enabling iterative refinement based on environmental feedback, overcoming the limitations of single-turn reasoning in handling complex scenarios.
current_progress.key_result: The proposed framework with multi-turn reasoning achieves a simulation-based trajectory-planning score of 96.2 (with ground-truth oracle) and 91.1 (with kinematic model), surpassing the human driving benchmark of 94.8 and significantly outperforming the single-turn baseline (83.7). Even with SFT training alone, multi-turn reasoning improves performance by 4.4 points (88.1 vs 83.7) compared to single-turn baseline, demonstrating the strong capability of the multi-turn reasoning paradigm.
current_progress.citation: Section 4.2 Main Results, Table 1

current_progress.text: Multi-turn GRPO, which computes turn-level advantages to address sparse reward problems, is more effective than standard GRPO with sequence-level rewards for multi-turn trajectory refinement tasks.
current_progress.key_result: Multi-turn GRPO with cross-turn normalization achieves a simulation-based trajectory-planning score of 96.2, outperforming GRPO with sequence-level reward (94.2) by 2.0 points and multi-turn GRPO with intra-group normalization (95.2) by 1.0 point. This validates that turn-level advantage calculation effectively addresses the credit assignment problem in multi-turn settings.
current_progress.citation: Section 4.3 Ablation Study, Table 3

current_progress.text: Single-stage training with combined single-turn and multi-turn data is more effective than two-stage training (first single-turn, then multi-turn) for developing multi-turn reasoning capabilities in autonomous driving models.
current_progress.key_result: Single-stage training with 80,000 single-turn and 50,000 2-turn samples achieves a simulation-based trajectory-planning score of 87.3, improving by 2.4 points over two-stage training (84.9) and by 3.6 points over the single-turn baseline (83.7). Adding planning-score understanding data and extending to 6 turns further improves performance to 88.1.
current_progress.citation: Section 4.3 Ablation Study, Table 2

current_progress.text: System-level optimizations — a streaming-based inter-process serialization mechanism and an intra-process tensor caching mechanism — can significantly reduce data transfer overhead in multimodal multi-turn RL training caused by high-resolution images and multi-turn sequences.
current_progress.key_result: The two combined optimizations reduce per-step training time from approximately 1250s to approximately 490s, achieving a 2.5× speedup. The streaming-serialization optimization contributes approximately 1.5× speedup and the tensor-caching optimization contributes an additional approximately 1.7× speedup.
current_progress.citation: Section 3.4 Multimodal Multi-turn RL Training System

rephrased_option_1: Integrate perception data into the vision-language model to enable self-generated feedback based on the model's own reasoning and enhance environmental understanding.
rephrased_option_2: Investigate methods to reduce the number of rollouts required for learning to increase the accessibility of the approach.

research_goal: To develop a comprehensive framework for distilling the collective reasoning capabilities of multi-agent systems into a single language model, thereby achieving the reasoning performance benefits of multi-agent collaboration while eliminating the computational overhead and vulnerabilities associated with inference-time multi-agent coordination.

current_progress.text: A single model can internalize the reasoning benefits of multi-agent systems without their high inference-time cost and collaborative vulnerabilities through structured distillation.
current_progress.key_result: The proposed framework significantly improves the performance of a single agent by 4.8% on average across benchmarks and achieves performance only slightly worse than vanilla multi-agent systems, while requiring only a single forward pass at inference time. The distilled models preserve the efficiency of one agent while exhibiting strong reasoning and self-correction performance of multiple agents.
current_progress.citation: Section 1 (Introduction), Section 4.2 (Main Results)

current_progress.text: Process-aware distillation using process reward models provides more effective and stable distillation than reasoning-enhanced supervised fine-tuning or data augmentation alone.
current_progress.key_result: Process-aware distillation consistently yields performance improvements across all datasets and settings, while reasoning-enhanced supervised fine-tuning and data augmentation show inconsistent gains that fluctuate by dataset. Process-aware distillation achieves the highest scores across all reasoning quality dimensions including step decomposition, intermediate verification, error localization, and overall reasoning coherence. Process-aware distillation demonstrates significantly more stable behavior across data scales, avoiding the sharp fluctuations observed with raw data scaling.
current_progress.citation: Section 4.2 (Main Results), Section 4.4 (Analysis of the Reasoning Quality), Section 4.3 (Scaling and Data Dynamics)

current_progress.text: PRM capacity matters more than student model size for effective distillation, while student capacity bounds the gains from multi-agent distillation.
current_progress.key_result: High-capacity PRMs enable strong improvements even for small students (e.g., 0.6B), whereas weak PRMs limit gains. For smaller students like a 0.6B model, scaling beyond 5 agents does not yield additional benefits and can lead to performance degradation due to limited representational capacity. In contrast, larger students like an 8B model benefit modestly from scaling to 10 and 20 agents, though with diminishing returns.
current_progress.citation: Section 4.3 (Scaling and Data Dynamics), Appendix C.1 (PRM Modeling and Policy Separation)

current_progress.text: Reasoning quality outweighs quantity: simply adding more reasoning trajectories does not improve performance, while high-signal process supervision yields stable gains.
current_progress.key_result: Across datasets, increasing training data does not lead to monotonic improvements. For both reasoning-enhanced supervised fine-tuning and data augmentation, performance exhibits high variance as data scale grows, with moderate data sizes yielding gains while further scaling often leads to stagnation or degradation. In contrast, process-aware distillation demonstrates significantly more stable behavior across data scales and consistently achieves competitive performance.
current_progress.citation: Section 4.3 (Scaling and Data Dynamics)

current_progress.text: Process-aware distillation improves reasoning behavior and internal consistency, not just accuracy.
current_progress.key_result: All distilled models achieve substantially lower reasoning perplexity than single-agent models, indicating improved predictability and coherence of reasoning steps. Process-aware distillation achieves the highest scores across all reasoning quality dimensions: step decomposition (3.23 vs 2.75 baseline), intermediate verification (4.07 vs 2.41), error localization (2.78 vs 1.97), and reasoning coherence (3.96 vs 1.88). Case studies demonstrate that distilled models acquire multi-agent reasoning patterns with more structured, logically coherent, and self-consistent reasoning.
current_progress.citation: Section 4.4 (Analysis of the Reasoning Quality)

current_progress.text: The proposed framework improves generalization and robustness to out-of-domain tasks and distribution shifts.
current_progress.key_result: On out-of-domain datasets spanning multi-hop QA, scientific paper QA, and multi-document summarization, the proposed framework significantly enhances cross-dataset reasoning transfer, particularly for larger models. For example, an 8B model improves F1-Score from 14.94 to 17.82 on the multi-document summarization benchmark. On a standard truthfulness benchmark, all distillation methods improve performance over the base model, with process-aware distillation achieving the highest scores across all metrics (BLEU: 0.6634 vs 0.6034 baseline), indicating resilience to catastrophic forgetting and better retention of factual correctness.
current_progress.citation: Section 4.5 (Robustness and Generalization), Appendix D.1 (Generalization on Out-of-Domain Datasets)

current_progress.text: Multi-agent distillation strategies transfer effectively across different model families and to multimodal LLMs.
current_progress.key_result: Cross-family distillation (e.g., across different open-source model families) yields larger and more consistent performance gains than same-family distillation. For multimodal LLMs, distilling from a 32B vision-language model to a 3B vision-language model from the same family using text-only reasoning datasets still improves performance, with process-aware distillation consistently achieving the strongest results, indicating that distillation captures model-agnostic reasoning patterns transferable beyond the training modality.
current_progress.citation: Section 4.2 (Main Results - Performance across Student Models), Section 4.6 (Distillation of Multimodal LLMs)

rephrased_option_1: Investigate adaptive ensemble scaling or selective trajectory sampling methods that optimize the trade-off between diversity and learnability in the distillation process.
rephrased_option_2: Develop efficient, predictive metrics for assessing data quality that do not require significant computational or resource overhead.

research_goal: The overarching research goal is to develop and validate a comprehensive, GPU-accelerated digital twin simulation framework for chemistry laboratories that enables the design, testing, and deployment of autonomous robotic workflows for accelerated materials discovery, thereby reducing reliance on costly real-world experimentation while facilitating sim-to-real transfer of complex, multi-step chemistry experiments.

current_progress.text: Digital twin simulation frameworks can effectively model multi-scale chemistry experiments by integrating physics-based simulation (robotic manipulation, particle dynamics) with a semantics engine (chemical kinetics, heat transfer, device functionalities) to enable workflow development and verification before real-world deployment.
current_progress.key_result: The framework successfully simulated two multi-scale chemistry workflows: (1) an organic chemistry experiment (a substitution reaction) demonstrating integration of robotic manipulation, heat transfer, and chemical kinetics, producing the target product at 1.08 mol/kg concentration after 2000 seconds; (2) a two-step reduction-oxidation experiment involving multiple solutions and reactions. The semantics engine added only 2.97% and 3.42% GPU overhead for 2048 and 4096 parallel environments respectively, demonstrating computational efficiency.
current_progress.citation: Results section, subsection "Simulation of Multi-Scale Chemistry Workflows" and Figure 5

current_progress.text: Workflows developed and validated in the digital twin simulation can be successfully transferred to real-world laboratory setups (sim-to-real transfer) for autonomous chemistry experiments involving general-purpose robots and automated platforms.
current_progress.key_result: Three real-world deployment experiments demonstrated successful sim-to-real transfer: (1) Object pick-and-place achieved 75% success rate across 12 trials with a beaker; (2) Liquid pouring achieved 90% success rate across 10 experiments, with poured volumes of 59.6 ± 7.9 mL (real) vs 61.3 ± 4.4 mL (simulation); (3) A long-horizon liquid handling station setup with a general-purpose robotic arm and an automated liquid handler executed successfully using policies developed entirely in simulation and transferred zero-shot to the real system.
current_progress.citation: Results section, subsection "Deployment to Real Laboratory Environments" and Figure 6

current_progress.text: A modular GPU-accelerated semantics engine can simulate behaviors not natively supported by physics engines (chemical reactions, heat transfer, device functionalities) while maintaining computational efficiency suitable for parallel reinforcement learning.
current_progress.key_result: The semantics engine successfully simulated: (1) heat transfer between heater, beaker, and solution with temperature-dependent reaction kinetics; (2) device functionalities including automated liquid handler tip loading/removal and liquid aspiration/dispensing; (3) chemical reaction kinetics with temperature-dependent rate constants using the Arrhenius equation. Performance testing showed GPU overhead of only 2.97-3.42% when adding heat transfer semantics to environments with 2048-4096 parallel instances, enabling efficient RL training.
current_progress.citation: Results section, subsection "Integrated Physics and Semantics Engine" and Figure 4; Methods section, subsection "Multi-Scale simulation: Integrated Physics and Semantics Engine"

current_progress.text: Combining hierarchical state machines with a library of reusable skills (based on classical motion planning, reinforcement learning, and imitation learning) enables flexible workflow design for complex, multi-step chemistry experiments without requiring hard-coded trajectories.
current_progress.key_result: The hierarchical state machine approach successfully enabled: (1) creation of abstract reusable skills like Pick&Place(obj1, obj2) by combining primitive actions (IK solver, grasping, motion planning); (2) execution of multi-step workflows including mobile robot sample transfer between workstations, liquid handling with the automated liquid handler, powder weighing and scooping, and multi-robot coordination; (3) integration of a 6D object pose estimator, eliminating the need for manual calibration and hard-coded positions. The approach was validated across multiple workstation environments and successfully deployed to real setups.
current_progress.citation: Results section, subsection "Skill Library Results: Manipulation and Perception" and Figure 4; Methods section, subsection "Workflow Plan Generation"

rephrased_option_1: Integrate quantum chemistry computational methods and machine learning to balance accuracy and efficiency for real-time simulation.
rephrased_option_2: Explore customized orchestration backends that support richer control flow and tighter integration with scientific compute environments.

research_goal: The overarching research goal of this project is to develop an efficient and adaptive video understanding framework that selectively applies chain-of-thought reasoning only when necessary, rather than unconditionally for all inputs. Specifically, this project aims to create a system that can automatically determine when explicit step-by-step reasoning provides genuine benefits for video understanding tasks versus when direct answering is sufficient, thereby achieving both high accuracy on challenging reasoning-intensive problems and improved computational efficiency on perception-oriented tasks.

current_progress.text: For RL-trained video models, direct answering often matches or even surpasses CoT performance, despite CoT producing step-by-step analyses at a higher computational cost.
current_progress.key_result: Analysis of three existing video reasoning models (covering general reasoning, temporal grounding, and spatio-temporal perception) showed that direct inference often matches or outperforms CoT inference on perception-oriented benchmarks for general and long video understanding, while generating significantly fewer tokens (e.g., 17.6 vs 386 tokens for the general-reasoning video model). Consistent CoT gains were primarily observed only on reasoning-intensive benchmarks.
current_progress.citation: Section 3.2 (Preliminaries - Analysis of Existing Video Reasoning Models), Table 1

current_progress.text: A think-aware dual-answer training paradigm that supervises both initial and reviewed answers eliminates the need for per-sample think/no-think labels and yields more stable training than traditional mode-switching approaches.
current_progress.key_result: The proposed dual-answer training approach achieved superior performance compared to training-based mode-switching strategies. The proposed framework consistently outperformed the training-based variant across benchmarks (e.g., 58.6% vs 55.7%) and avoided mode collapse issues, while the training-based approach showed unstable think ratios (e.g., only 1% on one benchmark vs 31% on another).
current_progress.citation: Section 4.1 (Method - Thinking Once, Answering Twice), Section 5.3 (Experiments - Comparison with Other Adaptive Reasoning Strategies), Table 7

current_progress.text: A confidence-based early-exit mechanism using length-normalized mean log probability of the initial answer can effectively determine when to invoke CoT reasoning, with the confidence score correlating with mode-switch accuracy.
current_progress.key_result: The confidence score of the initial answer strongly correlated with task difficulty: perception-oriented benchmarks showed high average confidence (>93%) with low think ratios (~25-39%), while a reasoning-intensive benchmark showed lower confidence (87%) with higher think ratio (51%). The mechanism achieved 94-100% recall on samples marked think-compulsory, i.e. where the initial answer was wrong but the reviewed answer was correct.
current_progress.citation: Section 4.3 (Method - Inference: Confidence-Based Early Exit), Section 5.3 (Experiments - Analysis of Confidence-Based Early Exit Mechanism), Table 8

current_progress.text: The proposed framework achieves state-of-the-art accuracy with significantly improved efficiency by reasoning only when necessary, reducing average response length while maintaining or improving performance.
current_progress.key_result: The proposed framework achieved state-of-the-art results across benchmarks while reducing average response length from ~149 tokens (RL with thinking baseline) to 44 tokens. On a reasoning-intensive benchmark, it improved accuracy from 54.7% to 58.6% (+3.9%) compared to a VLM baseline, and on a perception-oriented benchmark from 66.0% to 67.3% (+1.3%). The model adaptively activated thinking mode based on task complexity: 25% on a perception-oriented benchmark vs 51% on a reasoning-heavy benchmark.
current_progress.citation: Section 5.2 (Experiments - Main Results), Tables 2-4, Abstract

current_progress.text: Assigning higher weight to the reviewed answer (w₂ > w₁) in the dual-answer reward and including a fallback tolerance mechanism improves model performance compared to equal weighting.
current_progress.key_result: Asymmetric reward weighting (0.9:1.1 for w₁:w₂) with fallback bonus (α=0.3) achieved the best performance across three benchmarks: 67.3%, 58.6%, and 60.0 mIoU. This outperformed equal weighting (1:1) which achieved 66.1%, 56.1%, and 58.3% respectively. The fallback reward consistently improved results across all tested weight ratios.
current_progress.citation: Section 5.3 (Experiments - Ablation Study of Dual-Answer Reward Design), Table 9, Appendix Section on Reward Analysis

current_progress.text: Direct RL training without cold-start SFT on chain-of-thought data is more effective than SFT followed by RL for video reasoning tasks.
current_progress.key_result: Direct RL training achieved 66.1%, 71.2%, and 56.4% across three benchmarks. In contrast, SFT on the general-reasoning model's CoT data degraded the baseline (60.1%, 64.0%, 53.8% respectively), and SFT→RL performed worse than direct RL (61.7%, 64.3%, 53.5%). This demonstrated that low-quality CoT supervision can distort strong base models and create poor initialization for RL.
current_progress.citation: Appendix Section A.5 (Analysis of the Impact of Cold-Start SFT), Table 12

rephrased_option_1: Incorporate answer confidence calibration into the training objective so that the model learns to assign high confidence to direct answers for simple inputs and low confidence for complex inputs requiring multi-step reasoning.
rephrased_option_2: Investigate how reflection-like reasoning behaviors emerge and generalize by applying interventions during the training phase or incorporating supervision at intermediate reasoning steps.

research_goal: To develop a scalable framework for training language models on open-ended tasks by using interpretable model features as reward signals, demonstrated through the specific application of reducing hallucinations in long-form text generation.

current_progress.text: Features can be used as scalable supervision for open-ended tasks by transforming neural network features into reward functions that measure a model's belief (uncertainty) about concepts underlying desired behaviors.
current_progress.key_result: When applied to hallucination reduction in a 12B open-source instruction-tuned model, the proposed feature-reward RL pipeline achieved a 58% reduction in hallucination rate compared to the base model, at approximately 2 orders-of-magnitude lower cost (~$3,818 vs ~$344,064 for first 300 training steps) than using a closed frontier model as a reward model.
current_progress.citation: Section 1 (Introduction), Section 4.1 (Evaluating the Overall Method), Figure 2 (overall results)

current_progress.text: If a model learns the data distribution, it should be incentivized to represent latent variables underlying the data generating process as concrete features, which can be decoded using probes to provide well-calibrated beliefs about concepts like factuality.
current_progress.key_result: The classification probe achieved 0.94 AUC-ROC on validation data and maintained 0.85 precision and 0.56 recall at test time when applied to the trained policy's activations. The probes were found to be well-calibrated, with probe predictions closely corresponding to actual probabilities of the positive class.
current_progress.citation: Section 3.1 (Localize and Classify), Section 4.2 (Evaluating Individual Components - Probes), Figure 3 (probe evaluations)

current_progress.text: Training a policy to produce outputs that minimize the model's uncertainty (as measured by feature-based probes) about desired behaviors will result in the policy exhibiting those behaviors, without degrading performance on standard benchmarks.
current_progress.key_result: The feature-reward-trained policy achieved comparable performance to the base model across ten standard benchmarks spanning commonsense reasoning, science QA, math, multitask knowledge, and challenging reasoning. The policy also maintained similar numbers of claims per completion (63.4 ± 0.4 vs 63.6 ± 0.3 entities) and received similar preference ratings from a closed frontier model (50.9% preference rate).
current_progress.citation: Section 4.3 (Qualitative and Off-Target Effects), Table 1 (benchmark performance), Figure 7d (longform generation comparisons)

current_progress.text: Feature-based reward probes trained on a base model's activations will transfer to the trained policy's activations, enabling test-time compute scaling via Best-of-N sampling without requiring both models at inference.
current_progress.key_result: Fixed and retracted rates were essentially unchanged when the reward pipeline was run on activations from the trained policy versus the base model. Best-of-N sampling with the reward probes outperformed an LLM-as-a-Judge baseline by nearly 15 percentage points at N=256, and scaling N from 1 to 256 improved overall intervention success rates.
current_progress.citation: Section 4.2 (Test Time), Section 4.3.2 (Reward Pipeline Transfer), Figure 6 (test time scaling), Figure 7c

current_progress.text: The hallucination reduction achieved by feature-reward RL can be decomposed into three components: policy becoming less hallucinatory during training, in-context learning effects from inline interventions, and direct resolution of hallucinations through interventions.
current_progress.key_result: Of the 58% overall hallucination reduction: 10% came from the policy itself becoming less hallucinatory during training (policy reduction), 35% came from in-context learning effects when interventions were placed inline, and 13% came from interventions directly resolving hallucinations (direct reduction).
current_progress.citation: Section 4.1 (Evaluating the Overall Method), Figure 2 (pie chart decomposition)

current_progress.text: A two-step probing pipeline (localization followed by classification) is necessary to achieve reliable hallucination detection with high true-positive rates.
current_progress.key_result: The implemented two-step pipeline achieved 0.85-0.88 precision and 0.56-0.61 recall at test time with a fixed classification threshold of 0.7. The localization probe and classification probe working together were found to be critical for deriving a reliable probing pipeline with a high true-positive rate.
current_progress.citation: Section 3.1 (Localize and Classify), Section 4.2 (Probes), Figure 3a (monitoring pipeline)

current_progress.text: The trained policy exhibits larger distributional drift (measured by KL divergence) on hallucinated tokens compared to factually supported tokens, indicating targeted updates to model behavior.
current_progress.key_result: The average KL divergence between the base model and the feature-reward-trained policy was 10-20% larger on not supported tokens versus supported tokens, on both completions sampled from the base model and from the policy, though with large variation between seeds.
current_progress.citation: Section 4.3.2 (KL over Completions), Figure 7b, Appendix H (KL Between Base and Trained Policy)

rephrased_option_1: Apply the proposed framework to regulate additional model behaviors such as controlling agreeableness patterns and reducing output verbosity while maintaining task performance.
rephrased_option_2: Explore integrating learned reward agents into the multi-turn interaction paradigm and adapting the framework to work with agents in different simulation environments.

research_goal: To demonstrate that large language models can acquire complex reasoning capabilities through in-context learning mechanisms—specifically by learning compressed key-value cache representations (prefix tuning)—without modifying pretrained model weights, thereby overcoming the scaling limitations of traditional exemplar-based in-context learning while achieving comparable or superior performance to in-weight adaptation methods with greater efficiency in terms of data, parameters, and inference cost.

current_progress.text: Prefix tuning can enable LLMs to acquire complex reasoning capabilities without modifying pretrained weights and without overloading the context window, matching or surpassing in-weight adaptation methods.
current_progress.key_result: The proposed prefix-tuning method for reasoning matches or exceeds LoRA and supervised fine-tuning on challenging reasoning benchmarks: achieves 41.92% on a graduate-level scientific reasoning benchmark (surpassing both LoRA and full SFT), and outperforms LoRA on a grade-school math reasoning benchmark and a competition-style math benchmark while keeping all pretrained weights frozen. On two recent olympiad-level math evaluations, it preserves baseline accuracy (~13%) while in-weight learning degrades performance (~10%).
current_progress.citation: Section 4.1 (Experimental Results - Main Results), Figure 1 (teaser), Figure 3 (results_main_fig)

current_progress.text: The proposed method is more data-efficient than in-weight learning methods, requiring substantially less training data to achieve comparable accuracy.
current_progress.key_result: The proposed method requires 59% less data than LoRA to reach 50% accuracy on a grade-school math reasoning benchmark. It traces the Pareto frontier of accuracy versus training dataset size across nearly four orders of magnitude, matching ICL's data efficiency at low data while continuing to scale with additional data like in-weight methods.
current_progress.citation: Section 4.2 (Data Efficiency), Figure 4 (data_efficiency_plot)

current_progress.text: The proposed method is more inference-efficient than in-context learning, achieving better accuracy at substantially lower computational cost.
current_progress.key_result: The proposed method outperforms the best ICL configuration by 44.8 percentage points while using 90% less total inference compute on a grade-school math reasoning benchmark. On a graduate-level scientific reasoning benchmark, it achieves 11% higher accuracy than SFT while generating 34% shorter responses, directly reducing inference compute and monetary expense.
current_progress.citation: Section 4.3 (Inference Efficiency), Figure 5 (inference_cost), Figure 6 (verbosity)

current_progress.text: The proposed method is more parameter-efficient than LoRA, achieving higher accuracy with fewer trainable parameters.
current_progress.key_result: To reach a target test accuracy of 50% on a grade-school math reasoning benchmark, the proposed method requires 46% fewer parameters than LoRA. Under matched parameter budgets, the proposed method consistently dominates LoRA across the entire accuracy-parameter curve and continues to improve as the budget increases while LoRA saturates early.
current_progress.citation: Section 4.4 (Parameter Efficiency), Figure 7 (acc_vs_params)

current_progress.text: Prefix tuning can be strictly more expressive than LoRA due to an input-dependent carrier bottleneck in low-rank weight updates.
current_progress.key_result: A theoretical proof shows that prefix tuning is strictly more expressive when m > min{t_X, r}, where m is prefix length, t_X is context rank, and r is LoRA rank. Empirically validated: the proposed method increases the effective rank of learned representations by approximately 20% relative to SFT and LoRA. Learned prefix values place 70-90% of their energy outside the dominant value subspace used by the base model.
