current_progress.citation: Section 5 (Theoretical Perspectives), Theorem 5.1, Section 5.4 (Empirical Evidence), Figures 8-10 (effective_dimension, prefix_energy, effective_rank)

rephrased_option_1: Explore the proposed method as a post-training approach to acquire skills beyond reasoning capabilities.
rephrased_option_2: Explore methods that explicitly test generalizability and scalability, and incorporate them as optimization objectives.

research_goal: To develop a framework that automatically learns memory designs for agentic systems, enabling them to continually learn from past experiences across diverse domains without relying on manual engineering of memory components.

current_progress.text: Agentic systems can learn to continually learn by automating the design of their memory components through meta-learning, enabling memory designs to be specialized for diverse domains without relying on manual engineering.
current_progress.key_result: The learned memory designs achieved an overall improvement of 6.2% compared to the no-memory baseline with a smaller closed model variant and outperformed all state-of-the-art human-designed memory baselines across four sequential decision-making benchmarks. When transferred to a larger closed model variant in the same family, the learned designs improved the overall average success rate by 12.8% over the no-memory baseline and continued to outperform all human-designed baselines.
current_progress.citation: Section 4.4 (Results), Table 1

current_progress.text: Open-ended exploration is more effective than greedy search for discovering high-performance memory designs.
current_progress.key_result: On an embodied household-task benchmark, memory designs learned through open-ended exploration achieved success rates of 12.4% (with the smaller model variant) and 87.1% (with the larger model variant), compared to 11.9% and 77.1% respectively for designs learned with greedy search. The results demonstrate that exploration-driven learning processes outperform greedy search approaches.
current_progress.citation: Section 4.4 (Results), Appendix F.2

current_progress.text: Learned memory designs exhibit stronger scalability with increasing amounts of collected experience compared to human-designed baselines.
current_progress.key_result: In the embodied household-task benchmark, the learned memory design achieved higher performance faster with limited data and scaled better as more trajectories were provided during the memory collection phase. The learned design demonstrated both higher sample efficiency and stronger scalability compared to all four human-designed memory baselines.
current_progress.citation: Section 4.4 (Results), Figure 4

current_progress.text: Learned memory designs adapt more effectively than human-designed baselines under task distribution shift.
current_progress.key_result: When evaluated on the embodied household-task benchmark with memory collected from a seen split and tested on an unseen split under distribution shift, the learned memory design achieved a success rate of 84.1%, outperforming all human-designed baselines and demonstrating more effective adaptation under task distribution shift.
current_progress.citation: Section 4.4 (Results), Figure 5

current_progress.text: Learned memory designs are more cost-efficient than human-designed baselines while maintaining superior performance.
current_progress.key_result: The learned memory designs achieved an average success rate of 53.9% with lower overall end-to-end memory cost across all benchmarks ($0.09) and appropriate token sizes of retrieved content compared to all manual designs, effectively balancing memory efficiency and task performance.
current_progress.citation: Section 4.4 (Results), Figure 6

current_progress.text: Learned memory designs generalize robustly across different foundation models, with improvements not tied to a specific model.
current_progress.key_result: When the foundation model in the agentic system was changed from the smaller model variant (used during learning) to the larger model variant (testing transfer), the learned memory designs maintained superior performance over all human-designed baselines. The larger improvement observed as model-size tier increases (12.8% vs 6.2%, yielding a delta of 6.6%) exceeded those of all human-designed memory baselines, suggesting that learned memory designs provide stronger support as model-size tier increases.
current_progress.citation: Section 4.4 (Results), Table 1

current_progress.text: The proposed framework automatically specializes memory designs to the demands of different domains by discovering effective memory structures adapted to diverse task requirements.
current_progress.key_result: Visualization of learned memory designs showed that for games with explicit object interaction goals, the designs stored fine-grained knowledge such as spatial relationships and room layouts. In contrast, for tasks requiring complex reasoning, the designs favored domain-specific abstract strategies including strategy libraries and plan synthesis, indicating automatic specialization to domain demands.
current_progress.citation: Section 4.4 (Results), Figure 3

rephrased_option_1: Jointly learn the memory design and the agentic system itself to enable exploration of a larger design space and achieve better integration between memory design and the agentic system.
rephrased_option_2: Explore open-ended, real-world applications that involve more diverse toolsets and unpredictable task environments to validate the scheme's adaptability beyond standardized benchmarks with broader toolsets and more intricate reasoning chains.

research_goal: To develop and validate a multi-agent AI framework that leverages knowledge graphs and heuristic graph traversal algorithms to systematically generate cross-domain, evidence-grounded material design hypotheses for sustainable alternatives to PFAS (per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances), demonstrating that coordinated agent specialization in problem decomposition, evidence retrieval, design parameter extraction, and relational reasoning outperforms single-agent approaches in balancing creativity, traceability, and alignment with complex multi-objective design requirements.

current_progress.text: The multi-agent pipeline with all components enabled outperforms progressively simplified configurations across multiple evaluation criteria for hypothesis generation in materials science.
current_progress.key_result: The full multi-agent pipeline achieved the highest overall performance across six evaluation criteria (task decomposition, context enrichment, cross-subtask integration, deep reasoning, novelty, and source attribution), with scores of 9, 8, 5, 6, 7, and 7 respectively. The ablation study showed consistent decline in performance as components were removed, with the largest gap between the full pipeline and the minimal LLM-only setup. The full system demonstrated superior ability to balance nuanced retrieval, relational reasoning, and traceable evidence compared to all simplified configurations.
current_progress.citation: Section: Ablation Study (subsection 3); Table 2 (ablation-scores); Figure 3 (spider chart); Figure 4 (overall scores bar chart)

current_progress.text: Each specialized agent in the multi-agent framework contributes measurably and distinctly to the system's overall performance in hypothesis generation.
current_progress.key_result: Systematic removal of individual agents revealed specific performance degradations: removing the planner reduced task decomposition scores (from 9 to 6); removing the hybrid graph-traversal agent limited context enrichment (scores dropped from 8 to 1 in LLM-only); removing the creative graph-traversal agent diminished deep reasoning (from 6 to 3-4) and cross-subtask integration capabilities. The evaluator and creative graph-traversal agents working together strengthened cross-task reasoning, with direct graph exploration outperforming direct retrieval in deep reasoning. Systems incorporating external sources achieved higher source-attribution scores, with the full system scoring 7 compared to 1 for LLM-only.
current_progress.citation: Section: Ablation Study (subsection 3); specifically the paragraphs analyzing individual agent contributions

current_progress.text: Knowledge graph-guided traversal algorithms enable the discovery of novel cross-domain material connections and hypothesis generation that extends beyond direct literature associations.
current_progress.key_result: Different graph traversal algorithms (Shortest Simple Path, Top-N Shortest Simple Paths, Depth-First Search, and BFS with Semantic Stop) generated distinct material hypotheses with varying compositional complexity and property coverage. The Top-N Shortest Simple Paths (N=5) yielded a more multidimensional composite spanning polymer, bio-derived reinforcement, and inorganic filler components compared to the single shortest path's simpler bio-derived polymer composite, capturing broader property domains including mechanical flexibility, thermal stability, protein resistance, and surface biocompatibility. DFS approach explored deeper branches resulting in hierarchical composite architectures with layered functionality, demonstrating depth-prioritized accumulation of context along extended reasoning chains.
current_progress.citation: Section: Hypothesis Generation with Heuristic Knowledge Graph Traversal Algorithms (subsection 3.2); Figures 7-11 showing different algorithm outputs

current_progress.text: Semantic stops in graph traversal enable controllable hypothesis generation that aligns outputs with specific material families or design constraints while maintaining exploratory breadth.
current_progress.key_result: When the semantic stop was set to a target material family, the BFS algorithm successfully steered all keyword-pair paths to traverse through the target material, generating a hypothesis centered on a matrix of that material reinforced with functional nanoparticles and integrated into a gel system. This demonstrated that user-defined constraints can guide hypothesis generation toward specific material families (13 paths out of 91 node pairs included the target material) while still exploring complementary components through adjacent graph neighborhoods, enabling domain-critical outcomes while preserving relational exploration.
current_progress.citation: Section: Breadth-First Search (BFS) with Semantic Stop (subsection 3.2.3); Figures 9, 10, and 11

current_progress.text: Multi-agent systems with knowledge graph integration outperform direct LLM prompting for generating grounded, traceable, and contextually enriched scientific hypotheses.
current_progress.key_result: The LLM-only configuration scored lowest across nearly all evaluation criteria: task decomposition (4), context enrichment (1), cross-subtask integration (2), deep reasoning (3), and source attribution (1), compared to the full system's scores of 9, 8, 5, 6, and 7 respectively. While the LLM-only system showed comparable novelty (7), its outputs tended to be speculative and not grounded in external knowledge, whereas configurations with retrieval components achieved better balance between creativity and reliability, generating hypotheses that were imaginative but supported by retrieved information.
current_progress.citation: Section: Ablation Study (subsection 3); Table 2; discussion of LLM Only performance limitations

rephrased_option_1: Integrate the computational framework with autonomous synthesis laboratories to enable automated translation of generated material design hypotheses into physical prototypes.
rephrased_option_2: Extend the approach to resolve questions with conflicting evidence, similar to critical literature review tasks.

research_goal: To develop and evaluate AI agent systems capable of performing end-to-end ontology-based curation of scientific knowledge from large-scale literature corpora, specifically assessing their ability to autonomously search, extract, synthesize, and structure biological information from full-text scientific papers into standardized knowledge base annotations.

current_progress.text: Retrieval-based methods improve performance over parametric knowledge alone (memorization baseline), with gains varying by task type.
current_progress.key_result: At 16 papers, multi-agent achieves 53%, 53%, and 57% on gene ontology, expression, and synonyms respectively, compared to the memorization baseline's 49%, 35%, and 37%. Gains are significant on expression and synonyms tasks but relatively marginal on gene ontology, likely because GO terms are standardized vocabulary well-represented in LLM training corpora, while anatomical locations, developmental stages, and historical synonyms are organism-specific and less frequent in general text.
current_progress.citation: Section 5.2 (Experiment 1: Agent Architectures), Result 1

current_progress.text: Agent architectures with feedback loops (single-agent and multi-agent) significantly outperform fixed pipeline architectures at the same retrieval budget.
current_progress.key_result: On all tasks, single-agent and multi-agent architectures significantly outperform the pipeline baseline at the same retrieval budget. A key bottleneck for the pipeline is its inability to perform iterative ontology resolution: when batch resolution maps a description to an incorrect or overly generic term, there is no opportunity to refine the query. Agentic architectures can retry with alternative phrasings until finding the correct term, making feedback loops essential for ontology-grounded tasks.
current_progress.citation: Section 5.2 (Experiment 1: Agent Architectures), Result 3

current_progress.text: Multi-agent architecture with partitioned context outperforms single-agent architecture with accumulated context, especially at higher retrieval budgets.
current_progress.key_result: Multi-agent improves consistently across all tasks as paper budget increases from 1 to 16: from 37% to 53% on gene ontology, 37% to 53% on expression, and 39% to 57% on synonyms. In contrast, single-agent performance decreases beyond a peak on two of three tasks: on gene ontology, it reaches 50% at 4 papers then drops to 48% at 16; on expression, it peaks at 47% (8 papers) then drops to 45%. This pattern is consistent with context overflow—as retrieved documents accumulate in a single context window, relevant information becomes harder to locate amid noise. Multi-agent avoids context overflow by partitioning: each subagent processes one paper in isolation, keeping the orchestrator's context bounded.
current_progress.citation: Section 5.2 (Experiment 1: Agent Architectures), Result 2

current_progress.text: Scaling backbone LLM capability (from a smaller to a larger model in the same closed frontier family) yields diminishing returns compared to architectural improvements once models cross a capability threshold for agentic reasoning.
current_progress.key_result: The larger model achieves higher recall on gene ontology and expression tasks, but gains over the smaller model are modest (+2-4%) at 5× the cost. The 10-point gap between single-agent and multi-agent architectures significantly exceeds the 2-4 point gain from the smaller to the larger model, indicating that careful architecture design may offer more value than selecting a larger model. An older closed frontier model underperforms both, despite costing 7× more than the more recent smaller model, suggesting it lacks the complex reasoning required for effective agent loops.
current_progress.citation: Section 5.3 (Experiment 2: Backbone LLMs)

current_progress.text: Agents can handle missing ontology terms by finding alternative terms or proposing natural language descriptions, though they prefer to use existing terms rather than propose genuinely novel terminology.
current_progress.key_result: The agent achieves a semantic recall of 47.5% on missing-term group and 55.0% on available-term group. Much of the missing-term recall comes from approximate matches: agents predict parent or sibling terms when the exact term is missing. However, agents are weak at proposing genuinely novel terms. Of the 144 missing terms, the agent produced only 49 natural language descriptions, and these predictions have low semantic similarity to their targets (mean 0.20). Rather than describing unfamiliar concepts, agents tend to fall back on available ontology terms or skip the annotation entirely.
current_progress.citation: Section 5.4 (Experiment 3: The Missing-Term Setting)

rephrased_option_1: Explore interactive settings where agents retrieve candidate annotations for human expert review, request clarification on ambiguous cases, or learn from expert feedback.
rephrased_option_2: Extend the search agent framework to handle multimodal inputs including both text and images from scientific papers.

rephrased_option_1: Extend coverage of accessible literature by establishing institutional partnerships or expanding open-access corpus availability.
rephrased_option_2: Improve computational and retrieval efficiency to enable larger-scale expansion and utility of the knowledge graph.

rephrased_option_1: Integrate trainable cache representations into existing inference systems and post-training frameworks using mechanisms similar to standard caching approaches.
rephrased_option_2: Investigate dynamic compute allocation to balance reasoning effort across different reasoning stages in large-scale deployments.

research_goal: To develop and evaluate an automated system that can generate, implement, and execute research ideas for open-ended AI research problems, and to investigate whether large language models can learn to generate more effective ideas by grounding their ideation process in execution feedback through evolutionary search and reinforcement learning.

current_progress.text: Frontier LLMs can generate ideas that outperform provided baselines through best-of-N sampling, and a large fraction of LLM-generated ideas can be successfully executed automatically on open-ended AI research problems.
current_progress.key_result: Benchmarking experiments showed that frontier LLMs achieved high execution rates (over 90% for two frontier closed-source models on a popular pre-training task with self-execution), and best-of-N performance (N=50) beat baseline solutions. For example, one frontier closed-source model achieved 60.4% max accuracy vs 48.0% baseline on a conventional post-training task; another achieved 3.237 min loss vs 3.255 baseline on the pre-training task.
current_progress.citation: Section 3: Benchmarking LLM Ideators and Executors; Figure 2

current_progress.text: Execution-guided evolutionary search can find research ideas that significantly outperform baseline solutions on realistic LLM research problems (pre-training and post-training) within a limited number of search epochs.
current_progress.key_result: Within 10 search epochs, execution-guided search found a post-training recipe achieving 69.4% vs 48.0% baseline on the post-training task, and a pre-training recipe achieving 19.7 minutes vs 35.9 minutes baseline on the pre-training task to reach target validation loss. The post-training result outperformed the best human expert solution (68.8%) from a public class leaderboard.
current_progress.citation: Section 4: Execution-Guided Evolutionary Search; Table 1; Figure 3

current_progress.text: Execution-guided evolutionary search is more sample-efficient than best-of-N sampling under the same sampling budget.
current_progress.key_result: Comparison on the pre-training task with a frontier closed-source model showed that evolutionary search significantly outperformed best-of-N from epoch 1 onward under the same sampling budget (N=80 ideas per epoch), demonstrating that the model effectively leverages trajectories from previous epochs to generate more effective ideas.
current_progress.citation: Section 4.3: Comparison with Best-of-N; Figure 4

current_progress.text: LLMs generate substantial amounts of algorithmic ideas (beyond hyperparameter tuning) during execution-guided search, and algorithmic ideas achieve higher upper-bound performance than hyperparameter ideas in most cases.
current_progress.key_result: Quantitative analysis showed three frontier closed-source models generated substantial algorithmic ideas: 96.3%, 58.9%, and 95.0% respectively on the post-training task; 91.3%, 68.7%, and 84.6% on the pre-training task. Algorithmic ideas achieved the best performance for all models except the second.
current_progress.citation: Section 4.4: Analysis of Generated Ideas; Table 2

current_progress.text: Reinforcement learning from execution reward can improve the average reward of ideator models on open-ended AI research problems.
current_progress.key_result: RL training with GRPO on a 30B open-source model successfully improved average reward: on the post-training task, average accuracy increased from 0.253 to 0.343 after 40 epochs; on the pre-training task, average reward increased from 0.194 to 0.246 after 68 epochs (corresponding to average validation loss decreasing from 5.150 to 4.066).
current_progress.citation: Section 5: Reinforcement Learning from Execution Reward; Section 5.2: Experiment Results; Figure 5 (upper row)

current_progress.text: Reinforcement learning from execution reward improves the maximum reward (upper-bound performance) of ideator models on open-ended AI research problems.
current_progress.key_result: This hypothesis was invalidated. The max reward fluctuated throughout RL training without a clear upward trend on both the post-training and pre-training tasks, revealing a crucial limitation of the standard GRPO algorithm for improving idea generation where upper-bound performance matters more than average quality.
current_progress.citation: Section 5.2: Experiment Results, paragraph "The Case of Max Reward"; Figure 5 (lower row)

current_progress.text: RL from execution reward causes models to converge on simple, easy-to-implement ideas, leading to diversity collapse and shrinking thinking lengths.
current_progress.key_result: Analysis confirmed this hypothesis: (1) Thinking trace lengths rapidly decreased during RL training while idea lengths stayed constant; (2) Ideas with longer thinking had consistently lower execution rates; (3) Severe diversity collapse occurred - in the pre-training task, 119 out of 128 sampled ideas at epoch 68 were one of two common simple ideas (replacing RMSNorm with LayerNorm or performing EMA), compared to 51 out of 128 at epoch 0.
current_progress.citation: Section 5.3: Analysis of Training Dynamics; Figures 6 and 7

rephrased_option_1: Implement more capable coding agents with access to external tools and the ability to install new libraries in the execution environments, rather than directly prompting a language model for code modifications.
rephrased_option_2: Investigate how the proposed training scheme scales to larger parameter models to determine whether more powerful base models amplify the benefits of structured reasoning feedback.

research_goal: The overarching research goal is to develop a scalable and reliable paradigm for training autonomous GUI agents by shifting from passive, post-hoc verification to proactive, in-situ self-verification. Specifically, the work aims to create self-verifying agents that simultaneously learn to complete complex tasks and curate minimal, decisive snapshot evidence to prove their accomplishment, thereby reducing verification costs and improving reliability in agentic reinforcement learning.

current_progress.text: The self-verifying agent paradigm, which shifts verification from passive post-hoc analysis to proactive in-situ self-verification by the agent itself, enables scalable and reliable training of LLM-driven agents for GUI tasks.
current_progress.key_result: The proposed system achieves performance gains up to 26.08% and 16.66% respectively for 8B and 30B models across models spanning 7B-32B parameters from two open-source families on a standard Android agent benchmark. The trained agents (e.g., the proposed system with the 8B and 32B models from the same family) achieve competitive performance with much larger frontier models, demonstrating that the self-verification paradigm fundamentally enhances training efficiency and scalability.
current_progress.citation: Section 4.3 (Main Results), Table 2, Figure 1

current_progress.text: The three principles (completeness, conciseness, and creativity) for evidence curation create a synergistic learning loop where the requirement to find and present evidence compels the agent to develop deeper understanding of tasks, as the knowledge required for successful execution and effective proof are deeply intertwined.
current_progress.key_result: Fine-tuning with self-verification (following the completeness, conciseness, creativity principle) substantially outperforms vanilla fine-tuning using a standard Android instruction-tuning dataset across all models. For example, a 7B model achieves 30.15% SR with the proposed FT versus 20.28% with vanilla FT (+17.83% gain), and an 8B model achieves 26.81% versus 19.56% (+16.66% gain). The qualitative analysis shows agents learn to decompose tasks into actionable checklists and proactively create evidence-oriented actions (e.g., taking snapshots that list target transactions), demonstrating the synergy between task completion and evidence curation.
current_progress.citation: Section 4.3 (Main Results), Table 2; Section 4.4 (Qualitative Analysis, Case Study), Figures 7-8

current_progress.text: Grounding evidence in objective, unalterable tool interactions (action-observation pairs) rather than agent self-declarations prevents reward hacking and enables trustworthy verification, as agents cannot fabricate or manipulate the evidence.
current_progress.key_result: The RL training dynamics show consistent improvement in training reward with decreasing intra-group variance, indicating agents learn to complete tasks and curate valid evidence without gaming the system. The number of evidences converges toward 1.5 on average (satisfying the conciseness principle), and agents learn more compact interaction patterns (decreasing number of agent turns and response length over training). The evidence validity check shows agents successfully navigate toward relevant application screens even when tasks ultimately fail, demonstrating the robustness of the grounded evidence formulation.
current_progress.citation: Section 4.4 (Qualitative Analysis - Behavior Evolution and Performance Variation), Figures 5-6

current_progress.text: Intrinsic reward shaping through structured, dense feedback from an LLM/VLM verifier (including evidence validity, strict evidence-to-task grounding, format compliance, and conciseness penalties) accelerates the agent's acquisition of evidence curation skills during reinforcement learning.
current_progress.key_result: RL with the proposed system's reward shaping consistently improves performance beyond SFT across all model families. For instance, one 8B model improves from 23.91% (FT) to 31.15% (RL). Across three models from a different family, the 7B model improves from 30.15% to 30.43%, the 8B model from 26.81% to 36.23%, and the 32B model from 28.98% to 34.78%. The training reward curves show monotonic improvement with decreasing variance, and agents develop efficient behaviors (fewer turns, shorter responses, minimal evidence sets) that satisfy the reward shaping objectives.

rephrased_option_1: Extend the self-verifying agent paradigm to more diverse and heterogeneous task environments beyond the single benchmark currently tested, addressing the engineering challenges of scaling reinforcement learning with resource-intensive sandbox environments.
rephrased_option_2: Investigate multimodal reasoning approaches that allow models to dynamically revisit visual content during the reasoning process rather than reasoning solely from initial encoded representations.
